summary: Agentic AI is driving the next phase of AML innovation. U.S. firms deployed 1,200+ regulatory AI models in 2024. The RegTech market is projected to reach $60B by 2030 at 25% CAGR. 95-98% false positive rates in transaction monitoring create massive analyst burden. KYC/AML is shifting from periodic refresh to perpetual KYC. AgentDash's multi-model orchestration can cross-reference regulatory sources (OCC, SEC, FFIEC, FinCEN) that no single-model platform handles well, while governance layer provides the deterministic policy enforcement that SR 11-7 demands.

workflows.name: KYC/AML Transaction Monitoring
workflows.description: Agents analyze transaction patterns in real-time, score suspicious activity, suppress 95%+ false positives, and generate SAR-ready packages for confirmed alerts. Support perpetual KYC by continuously monitoring customer risk profiles.
workflows.currentPain: False positive rates in transaction monitoring remain 95-98% (Celent/McKinsey). Mid-size banks employ 50-200+ analysts at $5-15M/year just for alert review. SAR filing volume increases 10% annually while staffing stays flat.

workflows.name: Regulatory Change Management
workflows.description: Agents ingest regulatory updates from Federal Register, OCC bulletins, FFIEC guidance, and SEC releases. Map changes to institutional obligations, draft impact assessments, and flag policies that need updating.
workflows.currentPain: Thomson Reuters tracked 61,000+ regulatory alerts from 2,200+ bodies globally in 2023. Response time from regulation publication to institutional implementation averages 6-12 weeks manually.

workflows.name: Regulatory Reporting Automation
workflows.description: Agents aggregate data from multiple source systems, apply regulatory calculation logic, populate Call Reports (FFIEC 031/041/051), stress test documentation, and SEC filings. Generate variance explanations vs prior periods.
workflows.currentPain: Quarterly Call Reports require 200-400 staff-hours at community banks, thousands at larger institutions. DFAST/CCAR stress testing consumes 6+ months of annual work. Restatements trigger examiner scrutiny.

workflows.name: Model Risk Management (SR 11-7)
workflows.description: Agents maintain model inventories, monitor model performance and drift, generate validation documentation, and flag when models need recalibration. Auto-produce the documentation OCC examiners require.
workflows.currentPain: Banks maintain 100-1,000+ AI/ML models. SR 11-7 requires validation and ongoing monitoring for each. Model risk management teams are understaffed relative to model proliferation. Examiner findings on model governance are increasing.

workflows.name: Customer Due Diligence & Onboarding
workflows.description: Agents collect and verify identity documents, screen against sanctions/PEP/adverse media lists, assess risk ratings, and prepare due diligence packages. Support perpetual KYC with continuous monitoring rather than periodic refresh.
workflows.currentPain: KYC onboarding takes 20-30 days on average. Banks spend $50-500M annually on AML compliance staff. Large banks report 100,000+ overdue KYC reviews at any given time.

platformFit.rationale: AWS infrastructure strong for data processing but Bedrock's single-vendor model approach doesn't match the multi-source regulatory cross-referencing need. Cloud-only limits adoption in air-gapped trading environments.
Strong on IT/HR employee support but not built for financial regulatory compliance. No understanding of AML/KYC/SEC requirements. Wrong domain entirely.

No-code builder attractive for simple workflows but financial compliance requires deep domain knowledge, multi-system integration, and deterministic policy enforcement that Lindy's credit-based model can't deliver.

businessOutcome: A mid-size bank ($10-100B assets) deploying agentic compliance can expect: 60-70% reduction in false positive alert review (from 50,000 to 15,000 monthly human-reviewed alerts), 50% reduction in regulatory reporting preparation time, 80% faster regulatory change response (weeks to days), continuous SR 11-7 model monitoring replacing periodic reviews, and $3-10M annual savings in compliance staffing. The shift from periodic to perpetual KYC alone justifies the investment.
examples: Agentic AI drives next phase of AML innovation (FinTech Global, Feb 2026): Multi-step autonomous compliance agents are moving from POC to production in Tier 1 and Tier 2 banks.
54% of financial services firms deployed AI initiatives by January 2025 (S&P Global), up from 40% a year earlier. 1,200+ regulatory AI models deployed by U.S. firms in 2024.
RegTech market reached $12.82B in 2023, projected to $60.77B by 2030 at 24.9% CAGR — proving massive and growing demand for compliance automation.
Secureframe automated CMMC compliance with AI-generated SSPs (March 2026) — demonstrating appetite for AI-powered compliance document generation across regulated industries.
obstacles: OCC SR 11-7 model risk management requirements apply to AI compliance models — must demonstrate validation, monitoring, and governance
EU AI Act classifies AI in credit scoring and AML as 'high-risk' — requiring conformity assessments and human oversight
False negative liability: if an AI system misses a SAR-worthy transaction, the institution faces FinCEN enforcement
Legacy core banking system integration (Fiserv, FIS, Jack Henry) adds 4-8 weeks to deployment
Compliance officer skepticism: 'AI can't replace my judgment' mindset requires careful change management
Data quality issues: transaction monitoring effectiveness depends on clean, normalized data across disparate systems

marketSizing.tam: $22-28B — Global RegTech market in 2026, projected to $60B by 2030 at 25% CAGR
marketSizing.sam: $7-12B — AI-powered compliance specifically (KYC/AML, regulatory reporting, risk management) for financial institutions in North America and Europe
marketSizing.som: $350M — Mid-market banks and credit unions ($1-100B assets) seeking multi-domain compliance AI that can cross-reference regulatory sources and provide deterministic policy enforcement
marketSizing.source: Grand View Research RegTech report, Mordor Intelligence, MarketsandMarkets AI in BFSI forecast

playbook.currentState: Financial compliance is at an inflection point. U.S. firms deployed 1,200+ regulatory AI models in 2024, with majority in AML, KYC, fraud, and transaction screening (S&P Global). But current solutions are fragmented point tools: Alloy for KYC, ComplyAdvantage for AML, Workiva for reporting, IBM OpenPages for GRC — no platform orchestrates across the full lifecycle. False positive rates remain 95-98% in transaction monitoring. Compliance officers spend 80% of time on operational tasks (alert review, report compilation) instead of strategic risk assessment. The market is decisively shifting from periodic to perpetual KYC, from rules-based to AI-native compliance, and from single-model to agentic multi-model approaches. Banks spend $270B+ annually on compliance (Accenture) with staffing at 50-60% of total cost.

idealCustomerProfile.segment: Mid-market banks ($1-100B assets), credit unions with complex membership, wealth managers with cross-border obligations, and fintech lenders under increasing regulatory scrutiny
idealCustomerProfile.size: $1-100B assets, 20-200 compliance staff, 50,000-500,000 customer accounts, filing 500+ SARs annually
idealCustomerProfile.painIntensity: Critical — compliance staffing costs growing 15-20% annually while regulatory complexity compounds. Examiner findings on AI governance are increasing. 73% of compliance leaders cite inability to hire qualified staff as top risk (KPMG).
idealCustomerProfile.buyerTitle: Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) or Head of BSA/AML, with sign-off from Chief Risk Officer (CRO) and General Counsel
idealCustomerProfile.budget: $300K-$1.5M for pilot from compliance or operational risk budget. Production: $1-5M/year. Justified against $5B+ in annual industry fines for compliance failures.

entryWedge.workflow: KYC/AML False Positive Reduction — the highest-volume, most measurable pain point with immediate ROI
entryWedge.why: Every bank has this problem. 95-98% false positive rates mean analysts spend virtually all their time closing benign alerts. ROI is calculable in week 1: alerts suppressed × analyst hourly cost × hours per alert. No write access to core banking needed — read-only transaction monitoring data. Fastest path to budget justification.
entryWedge.proofOfConcept: Deploy AgentDash agents ingesting transaction monitoring alert feeds from existing AML system (Actimize, Verafin, SAS). Agents re-score alerts using multi-model analysis: one model for transaction pattern analysis, another for adverse media screening, a third for network analysis. governance layer enforces: never auto-close alerts above $10K threshold without human review. Run in parallel with human analysts for 30 days. Measure: true positive detection rate, false positive reduction, time per alert, SAR quality.
entryWedge.timeToValue: 6-8 weeks. Week 1-2: AML system API integration + transaction data normalization. Week 3-4: Agent training on institution's alert patterns, typologies, and SAR standards. Week 5-6: Shadow mode with side-by-side comparison. Week 7-8: Supervised production with compliance officer review of every auto-disposition.

successMetrics.baseline: 95-98% of AML alerts are false positives reviewed by humans
successMetrics.target: Agents auto-close 60-70% of confirmed false positives, reducing human review volume by 60%+

successMetrics.baseline: Variable quality depending on analyst experience and workload
successMetrics.target: Agent-drafted SARs with consistent formatting, complete narratives, and proper FinCEN coding — reviewed and filed by compliance officer

successMetrics.baseline: 200-400 staff-hours per quarterly Call Report filing
successMetrics.target: 80-120 staff-hours with agent-automated data aggregation, calculation, and variance analysis

successMetrics.baseline: 6-12 weeks from regulation publication to institutional policy update
successMetrics.target: 48-72 hours for automated impact assessment, 2 weeks for full policy update with agent-drafted language

successMetrics.baseline: 100,000+ overdue customer reviews at any given time (large banks)
successMetrics.target: Perpetual KYC: continuous monitoring reduces periodic review burden by 70%, eliminating backlog within 6 months

successMetrics.baseline: 6-8 weeks of manual audit prep per OCC/FDIC/Fed examination
successMetrics.target: Audit-ready compliance packages generated on demand in hours, with full model documentation per SR 11-7

riskAssessment.risk: AI system misses a SAR-worthy transaction (false negative), leading to FinCEN enforcement action
riskAssessment.mitigation: Never fully automate SAR decisions — agents recommend and draft, compliance officers approve and file. governance layer enforces: all alerts above configurable thresholds require human review. Track false negative rate weekly with manual sampling.

riskAssessment.risk: SR 11-7 model risk scrutiny — OCC examiners question AI model governance
riskAssessment.mitigation: AgentDash's multi-model roster with individual model documentation maps directly to SR 11-7 requirements. optimization engine provides continuous model performance monitoring. Pre-build examiner presentation package with model inventory, validation results, and limitation disclosures.

riskAssessment.risk: EU AI Act classifies the compliance AI as 'high-risk', requiring conformity assessment
riskAssessment.mitigation: Design for EU AI Act compliance from day one: document training data, maintain human oversight, publish transparency reports. Position as augmentation tool (human-in-the-loop) not autonomous decision-maker. Conformity assessment cost: $50-100K per institution.

riskAssessment.risk: Legacy AML system vendor (Actimize/Verafin/SAS) positions AgentDash as competitive threat
riskAssessment.mitigation: Position as augmentation layer, not replacement. AgentDash makes existing AML systems better by reducing false positives from their alert output. Partnership approach: 'we make your Actimize investment more productive.'

riskAssessment.risk: Data quality issues in transaction monitoring data undermine agent accuracy
riskAssessment.mitigation: Phase 1 includes data quality assessment and normalization. Agents flag data quality issues as part of their analysis — turning a risk into a feature. Partner with data quality vendors (Ataccama, Informatica) for enrichment.

regulatoryConsiderations: OCC Model Risk Management (SR 11-7/OCC 2011-12): AI models used in compliance must undergo validation, ongoing monitoring, and governance. Multi-model roster requires individual model risk assessments.

FinCEN BSA/AML Requirements: SARs must be filed within 30 days of detection. AI-assisted SAR drafting must maintain human oversight and final approval. FinCEN is modernizing BSA to explicitly permit AI-driven compliance.
SEC Predictive Data Analytics Rules (2024): Broker-dealers and advisers using AI must evaluate conflicts of interest. No 'AI exception' to fiduciary duties.

FFIEC Interagency AI Guidance (2024-2025): Covers governance, risk management, fair lending implications, and third-party risk for AI vendors. Examiners are incorporating AI-specific examination procedures.

EU AI Act (2024, phased to 2026): AI for credit scoring and AML classified as 'high-risk' — requiring conformity assessments, transparency, and human oversight. Extraterritorial reach affects US banks with EU operations.
NIST AI Risk Management Framework: Increasingly referenced by US regulators as baseline for AI governance. AgentDash should document compliance with NIST AI RMF from day one.
playbook.competitiveLandscape: Financial compliance AI is fragmented across point solutions: (1) Alloy — KYC identity decisioning ($1.55B valuation). 600+ banks. Strong in onboarding but not in ongoing monitoring or regulatory reporting. (2) ComplyAdvantage — AML data and transaction monitoring. Claims 70% false positive reduction. $100M+ raised. Good at screening but not at multi-step compliance orchestration. (3) Chainalysis — dominant in crypto AML ($8.6B valuation) but expanding to traditional finance. (4) IBM OpenPages — legacy GRC platform with Watson AI bolted on. Dominant in large banks but aging architecture. (5) Workiva — regulatory reporting and ESG disclosure. Strong in output formatting but no upstream analysis automation. (6) SymphonyAI/Ayasdi — ML-based suspicious activity detection. Claims 30-50% SAR productivity improvement. NO PLATFORM orchestrates across KYC + AML + regulatory reporting + model risk + regulatory change management in a unified agent factory. That's the whitespace. AgentDash's multi-model orchestration can assign specialized models to each regulatory domain while governance layer provides the deterministic policy enforcement that makes examiners comfortable.
playbook.pricingModel: Compliance buyers have large budgets but require clear ROI justification. Recommended: (1) Pilot: Fixed-fee assessment ($200-500K) covering AML system integration, 60-day false positive reduction measurement, and examiner-ready documentation of the pilot methodology. Frame as 'compliance effectiveness assessment' to access risk budget. (2) Production — AML: Per-alert pricing ($0.25-1.00 per alert processed) with monthly minimum ($15-30K). Declining cost at volume. (3) Production — Regulatory Reporting: Per-report pricing ($2-5K per regulatory filing automated) or annual license ($100-300K/year). (4) Production — Full Suite: Annual platform license ($500K-2M/year) covering AML, KYC, reporting, and regulatory change management. Justified against $5-15M annual compliance staffing costs. (5) Managed Compliance: For institutions that want to outsource — $20-40 per customer account per year for perpetual KYC monitoring.

deploymentTimeline.milestone: API connection to existing AML system (Actimize/Verafin/SAS). Transaction alert feed flowing. Data quality assessment complete.
Agents trained on institution's alert typologies, SAR standards, and risk appetite. governance layer policies set for threshold-based human review requirements.
Agents re-scoring alerts in parallel with human analysts. Side-by-side comparison metrics collected. Zero automated dispositions.
Agents auto-closing confirmed false positives below risk thresholds. All escalations and SAR recommendations reviewed by compliance officer.
Call Report data aggregation automated. Variance analysis generated. First automated quarterly filing prepared for review.
Regulatory change management active. Perpetual KYC monitoring launched. SR 11-7 model documentation automated. Full compliance lifecycle coverage.

buyerJourney.action: OCC examiner issues MRA/MRIA on AML program effectiveness, or SAR backlog exceeds 90-day filing deadline, or compliance staffing costs breach budget ceiling, or peer institution announces AI compliance deployment

CCO team evaluates options: hire more analysts (can't find them at $120-250K each), outsource to managed compliance provider (expensive, less control), or deploy AI compliance automation (emerging, promising)
buyerJourney.stakeholder: CCO, Head of BSA/AML, Head of Regulatory Reporting

buyerJourney.action: Evaluate 2-4 vendors. Key questions: Can it handle our AML + reporting + regulatory change needs? Is it SR 11-7 compliant? Can we explain it to examiners? What's the false negative risk?
buyerJourney.stakeholder: CCO, CRO, Model Risk Management, IT

buyerJourney.action: Present pilot to risk committee. Budget: $200-500K from compliance/operational risk. Success criteria: measurable false positive reduction, examiner-acceptable documentation, no false negatives.
buyerJourney.stakeholder: CCO, CRO, CFO, Board Risk Committee

buyerJourney.action: Shadow mode → supervised → production. Weekly reviews with BSA officer. Monthly examiner-readiness review. SAR quality audit by compliance team.
buyerJourney.stakeholder: CCO, BSA Officer, Model Risk, Internal Audit

buyerJourney.action: From AML alert triage → regulatory reporting → regulatory change management → perpetual KYC → model risk automation. Cross-sell to wealth management, insurance, and payments divisions.
